site.title: auto-refine demo
site.tagline: Optimize bounded artifacts against a trusted evaluator — and keep only the changes that survive cost, latency, and safety constraints.
site.proofNote: Current live page uses deterministic local proof examples. It is intentionally honest about being a proof-of-loop, while showing exactly what gets optimized and why candidates are kept or rejected.

whatGetsOptimized.label: Bounded artifact
whatGetsOptimized.value: A file or policy you allow the system to change
whatGetsOptimized.detail: Examples: a prompt file, a routing policy, a scoring rubric, a config.

whatGetsOptimized.label: Trusted evaluator
whatGetsOptimized.value: A command that produces measurable results
whatGetsOptimized.detail: The evaluator decides quality, latency, cost, safety, or any other metrics you care about.

whatGetsOptimized.label: Objective + constraints
whatGetsOptimized.value: Improve one thing without breaking the others
whatGetsOptimized.detail: Higher quality is not enough if the candidate is slower, pricier, or unsafe.

whatGetsOptimized.label: Rollback-safe loop
whatGetsOptimized.value: Bad candidates are discarded and reverted
whatGetsOptimized.detail: The incumbent only advances on accepted improvements.

decisionRules.title: KEEP
decisionRules.body: A candidate is kept only when it improves the primary metric and still satisfies all hard constraints.

decisionRules.title: DISCARD
decisionRules.body: A candidate is rejected even if it looks stronger on the primary metric, when it violates cost, latency, or safety rules.

decisionRules.title: ROLLBACK
decisionRules.body: After a discard, the mutable artifact is restored to the last accepted incumbent. The bad trial does not linger.

examples.title: Optimize a support-system prompt
examples.problem: The starting prompt is too generic. It produces weak answers because it does not ask clarifying questions, does not emphasize safety, and does not guide step-by-step help.
examples.objectiveSummary: Maximize answer quality while keeping latency <= 2000ms, cost <= $0.02, safety_failures == 0.
examples.optimizedThing: The engine edits the prompt text itself — not the model — and evaluates whether the new prompt produces better behavior under constraints.
examples.bestArtifact:
You are a support assistant.
Be concise.
Ask one clarifying question when needed.
Give clear steps.
Respect safety constraints.
examples.whyKept: This candidate raises quality dramatically while staying under latency and cost limits and keeping safety_failures at zero.

caseStudies.label: User asks for account-recovery help
caseStudies.baselineBehavior: Gives generic support language with no clarifying question and no structured steps.
caseStudies.bestBehavior: Asks one clarifying question, then gives concise recovery steps and keeps the answer within the safety rubric.
caseStudies.whyItMatters: The improvement is legible as an artifact change: the prompt now instructs clarification, brevity, and procedural help.

caseStudies.label: Tempting but bad candidate
caseStudies.bestBehavior: A verbose candidate hit the same quality score, but was rejected because it exceeded latency and cost constraints.
caseStudies.whyItMatters: This shows auto-refine is not just chasing a single score; it is optimizing under business constraints.

records.name: attempt-1
records.mutation: add concise safety + clarifying + steps rubric

records.name: attempt-2
records.mutation: verbose policy-heavy prompt that improves quality but violates cost/latency constraints

examples.title: Optimize a ticket-routing policy
examples.problem: The starting routing policy under-escalates sensitive tickets. It is cheap and fast, but it sends some risky tickets to the wrong place.
examples.objectiveSummary: Maximize routing quality while keeping latency <= 2000ms, cost <= $0.02, safety_failures == 0.
examples.optimizedThing: The engine edits the routing rules themselves — which categories go to auto-answer, specialist, or manual review — and scores the policy on a fixed ticket set.
examples.whyKept: This candidate fixes the dangerous routing mistakes without blowing the cost or latency budget.

caseStudies.label: Sensitive payment issue
caseStudies.baselineBehavior: The baseline policy may still route some urgent payment tickets too cheaply and too casually.
caseStudies.bestBehavior: The kept policy forces urgent payment issues into manual review, eliminating the risky path.
caseStudies.whyItMatters: This is a direct business rule improvement encoded in the artifact itself.

caseStudies.label: Over-escalation candidate
caseStudies.bestBehavior: A broader manual-review policy improved some accuracy but was rejected because average cost went above the allowed ceiling.
caseStudies.whyItMatters: This shows the optimizer is useful for operational policy tuning, not just prompt tinkering.

records.name: attempt-1
records.mutation: tighten routing for refund/security/payment while preserving cost bounds

records.name: attempt-2
records.mutation: over-escalate many categories to manual review

records.name: attempt-4
records.mutation: unsafe routing that skips secure review
